---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
#library(stargazer)
#library(kableExtra)
#library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, inverse

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size40[
Practice building, polishing and interpreting univariate analyses

- Script: 2023-02-17-Univariate_Practice-WDI.R

- Data: 2023-02-17-WDI_Data_Extract.xlsx
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. 



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06_1-WDI_Front.png')
background-size: 75%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

On Friday we began exploring data from the World Bank's WDI database.

- I'd like us to keep digging into this data today.

<br>

Two aims:

1. Practice building univariate analyses in R, and

2. Practice analyzing and interpreting the visualizations and statistics you are producing.

<br>

Before we get to work, let's talk about presenting visualization to professional audiences.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/06_1-bad_plot.png")
```

???

Ok, evaluate the quality of this visualization for me.

### What does it do well? What does it do badly?

<br>

Trick question! It does NOTHING good. NOTHING!

- Don't use 3-d if the 3-d provides ZERO new information

- Make the axis scales logical!

- I don't think these are continents...

- Where does this data come from?

- What do these bars actually show?

- Why are there drop shadows?!?!?

<br>

### Is everybody clear on this is an abomination?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Polishing your Visualizations**]]]

.size40[
1. Include an informative title

2. Add a figure label and caption

3. Identify the source(s)

4. Ensure the axis labels are clear and readable

5. Maximize the data-to-ink ratio

6. No chart junk!
]

???

Let's talk general rules of thumb when producing visualizations for a professional audience.

- This means producing high quality visualizations that would look good in any presentation, report, book, web post, poster, etc.

<br>

1. Include an informative title
    - EVERY visualization should have a title that tells the viewer the main point of the analysis
    - Don't force them to guess or to struggle to decipher the point, just tell them.

2. Add a label to the figure (with a figure number)
    - e.g. Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 3
    - Otherwise it's impossible to refer to it in the text
    - Plus a caption to explain what is being shown in the visualization

3. Identify the source(s)
    - Somewhere on the Figure or in the label you MUST make clear where the data came from

4. Ensure the axis labels are clear and readable

5. Maximize the data-to-ink ratio
    - As much as possible, the "ink" should be tied directly to data
    - e.g. No extra 3-d nonsense!
    - e.g. No big empty spaces

6. No chart junk!
    - It's super easy to add nonsense to your plot and much of it simply distracts from the central point of the visualization.
    - Focus on the central argument of the viz!

<br>

### Questions on these?

- **SLIDE**: Example...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

```{r, fig.align = 'center', out.width= '80%', fig.width = 8.5, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.618}
d2 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/WDI-2023-02/Wk6-WDI-Life_Expectancy_Data.xlsx") |>
  select(year = "Time", country = "Country Name", life_exp = "Life expectancy at birth, total (years) [SP.DYN.LE00.IN]")

d_world <- d2 %>% filter(country == "World")
d_other <- d2 %>% filter(country != "World")

d_other %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(country, life_exp), y = life_exp)) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    #geom_hline(yintercept = d_world$life_exp, color = "red", linewidth = 1.5) +
    #annotate("text", x = .25, y = 78, label = "Global Avg", size = 4, color = "red") +
    theme_bw() +
    coord_flip(ylim = c(60, 80), xlim = c(0,11)) +
    labs(x = "", y = "Life Expectancy (Years at birth)", title = "Life Expectancy by UN Region (2019)")
# Life Expectancies Vary Significantly Across the World
```

**Figure 1**: Global life expectancy for 2019 is taken from the World Bank's World Development Indicators (WDI) database and organized by UN region.

???

Let's call this level 1 of polished.

- The title is a clear, simple description
- The Figure label is good and includes the source info,
- The axis labels are clear
- There's no extraneous chart junk and no wasted space




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

```{r, fig.align = 'center', out.width= '80%', fig.width = 8.5, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.618}
d2 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/WDI-2023-02/Wk6-WDI-Life_Expectancy_Data.xlsx") |>
  select(year = "Time", country = "Country Name", life_exp = "Life expectancy at birth, total (years) [SP.DYN.LE00.IN]")

d_world <- d2 %>% filter(country == "World")
d_other <- d2 %>% filter(country != "World")

d_other %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(country, life_exp), y = life_exp)) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = d_world$life_exp, color = "red", linewidth = 1.5) +
    annotate("text", x = .25, y = 75, label = "Global Avg", size = 4, color = "red") +
    theme_bw() +
    coord_flip(ylim = c(60, 80), xlim = c(0,11)) +
    labs(x = "", y = "Life Expectancy (Years at birth)", title = "The lack of development in Sub-Saharan Africa is a tragedy in global politics")
```

**Figure 1**: Global life expectancy for 2019 is taken from the World Bank's World Development Indicators (WDI) database and organized by UN region.

???

Let's call this level 2 of polished!

- Added descriptive statistics (the mean) for context

- Added a title that conveys an important conclusion

### Make sense?

<br>

### Questions on polishing a visualization?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Assignment for Wednesday**]
]]

.size45[
**1) Create a high quality, polished visualization for these six variables:**
- WB Income
- gdp_per_capita
- measles_herd_immunity
- measles_immunizations_pct
- birth_rate
- death_rate
]

???

Today you have two jobs.

Job 1: Produce six HIGHLY polished visualizations

- The good news is, we've already worked on the first four in class!

<br>

### Questions on Job 1?

- If you think of cool things you'd like to add to a visualization, ask me!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Assignment for Wednesday**]
]]

.size45[
**2) Submit to Canvas an argument that answers this question:**

.center[**What is the state of global development in 2019?**]

- Paragraph form
- Critical analysis, not simple description
- At least three pieces of evidence (polished figures)
]

???

Before class you must submit an argument answering this question that:

- Is in paragraph form (e.g. not just bullet points, complete sentences and each paragraph makes a complete point)

- Is built on critical analysis, not just simple descriptions of what you made,

- Includes at least THREE pieces of evidence inserted in the text to support your claims (figures MUST be highly polished)

<br>

Note, the RStudio export image tool will let you copy images and then you can paste them into the Canvas discussion board.

<br>

### Questions on the assignment?

Let's get to work!


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[.content-box-blue[**Assignment for Today**]
]]

.size45[
**Submit to Canvas an argument that answers this question:**

.center[**What is the state of global development in 2019?**]

- Paragraph form
- Critical analysis, not simple description
- At least three pieces of evidence (polished figures)
]

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# Load readxl package
library(readxl)

# Input data as new object
data1 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/WDI-2023-02/2023-02-17-WDI_Data_Extract.xlsx")
```

???

1. WB Income
2. gdp_per_capita
3. measles_herd_immunity
4. measles_immunizations_pct
5. birth_rate
6. death_rate

### How did this go for you?

### - Any trouble copying images into Canvas?

### - Any trouble polishing the graphics? e.g. things you wanted to tweak but couldn't?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size55[**What does your variable alone teach us about the world in 2019?**]]]

.size45[
1. WB Income
2. gdp_per_capita
3. measles_herd_immunity
4. measles_immunizations_pct
5. birth_rate
6. death_rate
]

???

*Save plenty of time for new stuff!*

<br>

*Split class into 6 groups, 1 per variable*

Groups, in a few minutes you will present your critical analysis of this variable.

- Use this as a chance to show us a highly polished viz, AND
    
- To make sure everyone understands the difference between simple description and analysis

<br>

### Questions on the task?

Get to it!

<br>

*PRESENT each and DISCUSS*

<br>

World Bank Income Levels
- Low: < 1,045
- Low-middle: < 4,095
- Upper-middle: < 12,695
- Upper: 12,695




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.6, out.width='100%'}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "green3", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "GDP per Capita", y = "Countries", title = "Global Wealth (2019)")

data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = birth_rate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "red3", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Birth Rate (per 1,000)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Opportunity (2019)")
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.6, out.width='100%'}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Measles Immunizations (% under 2 years)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Health (2019)")

data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = death_rate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "red3", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Death Rate (per 1,000)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Opportunity (2019)")
```
]

???

Taking all of this on board, where do we end up?

### Based on this data and these analyses, what would you argue is the current state of global development?

### - High confidence conclusions?

### - Low confidence conclusions?

<br>

Personal reflection time!

This exercise represents the kind of exercise I want you all to get really good at doing this semester.

- Be presented with a question, collect and clean data, apply statistical tools to the data and then use your findings to make a convincing argument.

### What do you need to do to get better at this exercise?

<br>

**SLIDE**: As I think you've seen from this exercise that univariate tools can be hard to interpret without context.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 1: Benchmark the Data**]]]

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.9, out.width='100%', fig.width=5}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_herd_immunity)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "blue") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Herd Immunity", "At Risk")) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count of Countries", title = "Global Health (2019)")
```

]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.9, out.width='100%', fig.width=5}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Measles Immunizations (% under 2 years)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Health (2019)")
```
]

???

The first way we can add context to our analyses is with a "benchmark"

- e.g. Can we define "good" vs "bad" values?

<br>

### Why does having both of these visualizations make our analyses so much more useful?

- (**SLIDE**: Better titles!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 1: Benchmark the Data**]]]

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.9, out.width='100%', fig.width=5}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_herd_immunity)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "blue") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Herd Immunity", "At Risk")) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count of Countries", title = "Too many countries remain vulnerable \n to measles outbreaks (< 95% vaccinated)")
```

]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.9, out.width='100%', fig.width=5}
data1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Measles Immunizations (% under 2 years)", y = "Countries", title = "The level of vulnerability in some countries is\n staggeringly high")
```
]

???

### Does everybody see from these the power of a title that makes the "point" of the analysis clear?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 2: Make a Map**]]]

```{r, echo=FALSE, comment=NULL, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide'}
# Measles data context: Make a map

# Load the package
library(rworldmap)

# Merge your data onto the world map
data2 <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data1, joinCode = "NAME", nameJoinColumn = "country")
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=.5, out.width='95%', fig.width=12}
# Output the map
mapCountryData(data2, nameColumnToPlot = "measles_herd_immunity", colourPalette = c("red3", "blue3"), mapTitle = "Countries Most At Risk of Measles (2019)")
```

???

The second way we can add context to our analyses is with a map.

- Geographic information can show patterns in the spatial layout

<br>

### What does this map add to our analysis of measles risk?

- (**SLIDE**: Better title!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 2: Make a Map**]]]

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=.5, out.width='95%', fig.width=12}
# Output the map
mapCountryData(data2, nameColumnToPlot = "measles_herd_immunity", colourPalette = c("red3", "blue3"), mapTitle = "Measles risk does not clearly overlap with standard rich-poor or north-south divides")
```

???

### What's another title that draws out other important analyses from this map?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 3: Across Time Data**]]]

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.85, out.width='100%'}
# Input across time data
data1b <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/WDI-2023-02/WDI-Measles_Across_Time.xlsx")

data1b |>
  filter(year == 1985) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Measles Immunizations (% under 2 years)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Health (1985)") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.85, out.width='100%'}
data1b |>
  filter(year == 2019) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Measles Immunizations (% under 2 years)", y = "Countries", title = "Global Health (2019)") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))
```
]

???

The third way we can add context to our analyses is with across time data

- Can we compare observations to themselves at different points in time?

<br>

### Why does having both of these visualizations make our analyses so much more useful?

<br>

### What's a better title for these plots?

- (The world has made incredible progress in fighting measles but there is much left to do!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size50[**Option 3: Across Time Data**]]]

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618, fig.width=8, out.width='80%', cache=TRUE}
# Measles data context: Animate the data

# Load the package
library(gganimate)

# Histogram across time
data1b |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  transition_time(year) +
  labs(title = "Measles Immunizations ({as.integer(frame_time)})")
```

???

R will also let us add a lot more time context with an animation!

### What does this help us see in the data?

<br>

Ok, let's learn to build all these things!

- You've already made the benchmark plots so let's move to the map!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**Option 2: Let's Make a Map!**]]

<br>

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Install the rworldmap package (only once per computer)
install.packages("rworldmap")

# Load the package
library(rworldmap)

# Merge your data onto the world map
data2 <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data1, joinCode = "NAME", nameJoinColumn = "country")

# Output the basic map
mapCountryData(data2, nameColumnToPlot = "measles_herd_immunity")

# Tweak the map
mapCountryData(data2, 
               nameColumnToPlot = "measles_herd_immunity",
               colourPalette = c("blue", "red"),
               mapTitle = "Title Here")
```
]

???

This isn't the only way to make a map in R, but it is the easiest way I've found.

- R has much more powerful mapping packages but this is a handy way to do quick analyses that still look pretty darn good.

<br>

*Walk them through the arguments in each function*

- Basically, each mapping package tells R the lines for the map and you just need to merge your data onto those map lines

- rworldmap is handy because it tries to automate this process for you

- The trade-off is that it has fewer tweakable options and if it doesn't understand your data it just omits it (e.g. country name mispelled)

<br>

*Encourage the class to try other variables in the nameColumnToPlot argument*

- Numeric variables are presented in 6 quantiles (e.g. 16% per color)

<br>

### Any questions on using rworldmap to make a world map?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**Option 3: Let's Filter a Histogram!**]]

.code110[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Input the data
data_all <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/WDI-2023-02/WDI-Measles_Across_Time.xlsx")

# Create two new data sets by year
data_1985 <- filter(data_all, year == 1985)
data_2019 <- filter(data_all, year == 2019)

# Histogram 1985
ggplot(data_1985, aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram()

# Histogram 2019
ggplot(data_2019, aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram()
```
]

???

*Encourage them to try other years in the data set*



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**Option 3: Let's Animate a Bar Plot!**]]

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Install animation package (one time)
install.packages("gifski")
install.packages("gganimate")

# Load the package
library(gifski)
library(gganimate)

# Histogram across time
ggplot(data_all, aes(x = measles_immunizations_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  transition_time(year) +
  labs(title = "Measles Immunizations ({as.integer(frame_time)})")
```
]

???

The key here is the "transition_time" function

- THAT's the piece that creates the animation as a gif

<br>

The wacky code in the title is just to mae the years appear cleanly to track the progress of the animation.

<br>

### Questions on these new techniques?

- Benchmarks, mapping, filtering across time data and basic animation?






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size70[**For Friday**]

.size50[
1. Healy (2019) Chapter 8 “Refine your plots”

2. Freedom House. (2022). *Freedom in the World: Methodology*.
]

???

Friday we'll practice all your new tools by exploring the Freedom House Freedom in the World data set.
